mūla.devanāgarī: अपरं भवतो जन्म परं जन्म विवस्वतः | कथम् एतद् विजानीयां त्वम् आदौ प्रोक्तवान् इति
mūla.iast: aparaṃ bhavato janma paraṃ janma vivasvataḥ | katham etad vijānīyāṃ tvam ādau proktavān iti
mūla.chapter_position: Chapter 4 (Jñāna-Karma-Sannyāsa-Yoga (The Yoga of Renunciation of Action in Knowledge)), verse 4
mūla.speaker: Krishna

senses_attested_in_panel.sense: । परं पूर्वं सर्गादौ जन्म उत्पत्तिः विवस्वतः आदित्यस्य। तत् कथम् एतत् विजानीयाम् अविरुद्धार्थतया यः त्व मेव आदौ प्रोक्त
परं प्राक्कालीनं विवस्वतो जन्म तस्मात्तवाधुनिकत्वाच्चिरंतनाय विवस्वते त्वमादौ योगं प्रोक्तवानित्येतत्कथमहं विजानीयां ज्
। परं पूर्वं सर्गादौ जन्म उत्पत्तिः विवस्वतः आदित्यस्य। तत् कथम् एतत् विजानीयाम् अविरुद्धार्थतया यः त्व मेव आदौ प्रोक्त
परं प्राक्कालीनं विवस्वतो जन्म तस्मात्तवाधुनिकत्वाच्चिरंतनाय विवस्वते त्वमादौ योगं प्रोक्तवानित्येतत्कथमहं विजानीयां ज्
एव आदौ प्रोक्तवान् इति कथम् एतद् असम्भावनीयं विशेषण यथार्थं जानीयाम्
इति कथम् एतद् असम्भावनीयं विशेषण यथार्थं जानीयाम्

advaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita.english_rendering: Arjuna, speaking from within the veil of body-identification (deha-abhimāna), presents the apparent contradiction: your birth in Vasudeva's house is recent (apara), while Vivasvat's birth was prior (para), at the beginning of creation — how can I comprehend that you, the later-born, taught the former? Śaṅkara reads this as a question posed to expose a seeming logical impossibility (aviruddha-artha-tā), which the Lord will resolve by revealing that the ātman — free of all birth — is the true speaker. The question is thus not about two men across time but about the confusion between the body that appears in time and the witness that never does.

viśiṣṭādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
viśiṣṭādvaita.english_rendering: Rāmānuja's Arjuna is not confused but is a skilled questioner: he knows perfectly well that Kṛṣṇa is the supreme Lord (sarvešvara) whose birth Bhīṣma and the ṛṣis have proclaimed, and he has heard at the Rājasūya that the entire moving and unmoving world exists as śeṣa (dependent remainder) of Kṛṣṇa. He asks nonetheless, because he wants the Lord himself to unfold — in precise theological detail — the nature of his voluntary descent: is it real or illusory (māyā)? What is the form of such a body? What is its cause? For what purpose? The question is a structured theological interrogation, not a doubt.

dvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
dvaita.english_rendering: For Madhva the question is compressed into a single purpose: Arjuna, having just heard 'fix all actions in me' (3.30), now wishes to know the Lord's greatness (māhātmya) from its very beginning — and so he asks about the primordial teaching to Vivasvat. This is not philosophical puzzlement; it is the jīva (eternally distinct from Hari) asking for testimony to Hari's sovereign priority over all time. The question is an act of devotional inquiry directed at establishing the ādikartṛtva (primordial agency) of Bhagavān. Madhva's commentary is terse because the metaphysical answer is already implicit: Hari alone is the unborn teacher behind every time-bound appearance.

śuddhādvaita.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
śuddhādvaita.english_rendering: Vallabha reads the question as Arjuna's hidden desire to hear the avatāra-yāthātmya — the true nature of divine descent as Kṛṣṇa's own līlā (play). Arjuna uses the 'impossibility' of Vivasvat's teaching as a pretext (miṣa) to invite the Lord to reveal his eternal self. The birth in Vasudeva's house appears recent (arvācīna) only to those whose vision is bounded by sequential time; the question therefore opens the space for Kṛṣṇa to disclose the prasāda-rūpa (grace-form) that stands behind all temporal succession.

bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
bhakti.english_rendering: Śrīdhara Svāmī states plainly: Arjuna, seeing the impossibility (asambhava) of the Lord teaching Vivasvat, poses this question. Your birth is recent (arvācīna); Vivasvat's was long prior (prākkālīna); therefore how can I be capable of knowing (jñātum śaknuyām) that the more recent-born taught the more ancient? The voice is philological and measured: the question is about chronological sequence, and the answer will hinge on the distinction between body-time and ātman-time. Śrīdhara anticipates Kṛṣṇa's reply by using jñātum śaknuyām — 'how could I be capable of knowing?' — pointing to an epistemic deficiency that only divine self-disclosure can cure.

advaita-bhakti.reading_summary: (reading summary extraction pending; ENABLE_READING_SUMMARIES=true to generate)
advaita-bhakti.english_rendering: Madhusūdana Sarasvatī sees Arjuna's question as performing a dual service for the audience: it articulates the doubt of the foolish (mūrkhāṇām) who take Kṛṣṇa to be a mortal (manuṣyatva) and therefore non-omniscient (asarvajña) and impermanent. He names two interlocking objections — asarvajñatva (not all-knowing) and anityatva (not eternal) — and shows they collapse into each other: a body-bound being cannot remember prior births, and this body did not exist at creation's dawn. The depth of Arjuna's question lies in its dialectical structure: neither 'another birth' (janmāntara) nor 'this very body' (idānīm-tana deha) can dissolve the contradiction — only a third ontological mode, which Kṛṣṇa will supply, can.

corpus_provenance.mūla: Belvalkar critical edition (BORI 1947), via Ambuda multi-witness

so_what_questions: Why does Arjuna ask about this now — after three chapters of ethical and metaphysical instruction — rather than at the outset?
Is Arjuna genuinely confused, or is this a Socratic move designed to elicit a self-disclosure from Kṛṣṇa?
What does it mean for a teaching (yoga) to be transmitted across cosmic time — does the content of the yoga change, or only its vehicle?
The verse uses 'apara' and 'para' (recent and prior) — does this temporal pair carry any connection to the Upaniṣadic apara/para vidyā distinction (lower/higher knowledge)?
If Kṛṣṇa's answer will be 'I have many births but remember them all' (4.5), what does that claim require us to accept about the relationship between memory, identity, and the body?
How do the six schools differ in whether they read Arjuna as confused (advaita, Śrīdhara) versus strategically asking (Rāmānuja, Vallabha)?
What is at stake philosophically in the distinction between 'teaching in a past body' versus 'teaching as the unchanging ātman clothed in successive bodies'?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you find yourself saying 'I am not the same person I was ten years ago' and using that change as an excuse not to honour a commitment, notice the confusion: the body and personality shift, but the witness-awareness (sākṣin) that knows all those changes is continuous. The Advaita reading asks: who is the 'I' across the time-gap — the body or the aware presence behind it?
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: Before raising a hard question with someone you love or respect, examine whether your question is actually an invitation for them to reveal themselves more fully. Rāmānuja's Arjuna already knows the answer; he asks because the asking itself is an act of relational kainkarya (devoted service) that allows the beloved to speak. Use questions as offerings, not just information requests.
everyday_applications.dvaita: Madhva's compressed reading is a reminder that the purpose of asking about great teachers' origins is not intellectual curiosity but recognition of their primordial authority. When you study any tradition, orient your first questions toward 'what is the source of this person's authority?' rather than 'does this make logical sense to me?' — the māhātmya question precedes the epistemological one.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: Vallabha's Arjuna uses an 'impossible' situation as a pretext to invite the beloved to speak. In relationships, sometimes the most generative thing you can offer is a question you already half-know the answer to — a question whose real function is to create the space for the other to be fully, joyfully themselves. The 'pretext question' is an act of grace.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Śrīdhara's Arjuna confesses epistemic limitation plainly: 'how could I be capable of knowing?' (jñātum śaknuyām). This is the appropriate posture before a teacher whose knowledge exceeds our category of time. Cultivate the habit of stating your confusion as a specific incapacity rather than a general scepticism — not 'I don't believe you' but 'I cannot yet see how this fits together; please show me.'
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: Madhusūdana identifies two compounding errors: thinking the teacher is bound by time (anityatva) and therefore forgetful (asarvajñatva). In modern terms: we discount advice from someone older or younger than ourselves on the grounds that 'they can't know what my world is like.' But wisdom that has survived multiple contexts is precisely the wisdom least bound by any one context. Test your dismissals: are they tracking a real limitation in the teacher, or projecting a limitation you assume time imposes?
